cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Implement `willSet` and `didSet` observers to print the upcoming and previous values of a property during a change.
expectedOutput:
Will set to 2
Did set from 1
hints: Property observers allow you to run code whenever a property is set.
Use 'newValue' inside 'willSet' to access the value that is about to be assigned.
Use 'oldValue' inside 'didSet' to access the value that was replaced.
The exact print format is: "Will set to \(newValue)" and "Did set from \(oldValue)".
title: Property Observers

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
hints: Lazy properties are created the first time they are accessed.
A lazy property must always be declared as a variable (using 'var'), because its initial value might not be retrieved until after instance initialization completes.
The value of a lazy property is only calculated once, the first time it is used.
lesson:
Lesson: Lazy Properties
A lazy stored property is a property whose initial value is not calculated until the first time it is used. This is useful for properties whose initial values are expensive to compute or depend on external factors.

Key rules:
1. You must always declare a lazy property as a variable (using the 'var' keyword).
2. Lazy properties are useful for deferring work until it's actually needed.

Example:
    struct DataManager {
        lazy var importer = DataImporter() // Only created when accessed
        var data: [String] = []
    }

Note: If a property marked with 'lazy' is accessed by multiple threads simultaneously and the property has not yet been initialized, there is no guarantee that the property will be initialized only once.
title: Lazy Property

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Use static properties to share values across instances
hints: Static properties live on the type itself, not on any one instance.
Access a static property by prefixing it with the type name: 'Shift.maxHours'.
Instance properties are accessed via an instance of the type: 'shift.hours'.
lesson:
Lesson: Type Properties (static)
Properties usually belong to instances of a type. However, you can also define 'type properties' that belong to the type itself.

Core Concepts:
1. static keyword: Marks a property as belonging to the type. There is only ever one copy, regardless of how many instances you create.
2. Access: Use the type name (e.g., 'Shift.maxHours') to read or write type properties.
3. Universal Values: Use static properties for constants or variables that should be shared by every instance (like a max limit, a shared cache, or a default configuration).

Example:
    struct Settings {
        static let apiVersion = "1.0"
        var username: String
    }
    print(Settings.apiVersion) // Access via Type
title: Static vs Instance

hints: Protocols define required properties or methods.
A read-only property in a protocol is defined as 'var status: String { get }'.
Protocols do not have curly braces for implementations, just the signature followed by a semicolon or line break (for methods) or the { get } / { get set } requirements.
title: Protocol Definition

description: Adopt the `Inspectable` protocol in a `Furnace` struct and print its status as "Ready".
hints: The struct 'Furnace' should be declared as 'struct Furnace: Inspectable'.
To satisfy '{ get }', you can use either 'let' or 'var' in your struct.
Ensure you create an instance with the status "Ready" to match the expected output.
title: Conformance

description: Use the `HeatSource` protocol as a parameter type in a function and call it with a `heat` value of `1200`.
hints: The parameter 'source' in your function should be of type 'HeatSource', not the concrete 'Burner'.
Because the function accepts the protocol, it can access any properties defined in that protocol, such as 'heat'.
Create a Burner with a heat value of 1200 to satisfy the output check.
title: Protocol as Type

description: Write a function that requires a parameter to conform to both `Fueling` and `Venting` using protocol composition (`&`).
hints: Use protocol composition (`&`) to require both capabilities in a single parameter.
The type of the parameter should be 'Fueling & Venting'.
Inside the function, you can access properties from both protocols on the single parameter.
Call the function with a 'Vent' instance (fuel: 2, airflow: 3) to match the output.
lesson:
Lesson: Protocol Composition
Protocol composition allows you to require a type to conform to multiple protocols simultaneously without defining a new inherited protocol. You do this using the ampersand (&) operator.

Core Concepts:
1. Ad-hoc Requirements: Composition is useful when a function needs several independent behaviors (e.g., 'Encodable & Decodable').
2. Flexibility: It avoids creating deep protocol inheritance hierarchies for simple combinations of requirements.

Example:
    func process(item: Named & Codable) {
        print("Processing \(item.name)")
    }

Note: You can combine as many protocols as needed using multiple & operators.
title: Protocol Composition

description: Create a new protocol `InspectableComponent` that inherits from `Component`, then conform a `Sensor` struct to it and print its status.
hints: Protocol inheritance uses the same colon syntax as class inheritance: 'protocol Child: Parent'.
To inherit from another protocol, use a colon after your new protocol name followed by the parent protocol name.
Types that conform to 'InspectableComponent' must implement requirements from both it AND the 'Component' protocol.
Create an instance with a status of "OK" to pass the output check.
title: Protocol Inheritance

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Extend the `Ingot` struct with a read-only computed property `label` that returns the string "Ingot weight: " followed by the weight, then print the label for an ingot with weight 5.
hints: Use 'extension Ingot { ... }' to add the new property.
A read-only computed property can be declared using shorthand: 'var label: String { ... }'.
The property should return "Ingot weight: \(weight)".
Ensure you use exactly "Ingot weight: " (with a space after the colon) to match the output check.
Create an Ingot instance with weight 5 and print its label.
title: Extensions

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Provide a default implementation for a protocol method that uses an internal property to format a message, then call it on a struct with the status "Ready".
hints: Use 'extension Reportable { ... }' to provide a default implementation for 'printReport()'.
Important: In Swift, you cannot write a method body inside the protocol definition itself; it must go in an extension.
Because of the default implementation, any struct that conforms to 'Reportable' will have 'printReport()' automatically.
The method should print the prefix "Report status: " followed by the value of the 'message' property.
Ensure you create your struct instance with the message "Ready" to match the output check.
title: Default Implementations
